executiveSummary.text: Duke Energy is a regulated utility operating across the Carolinas, Florida, Ohio, Indiana, and Kentucky; it offers a defensive 3.5% dividend yield supported by rate base growth from grid modernisation, but limited capital appreciation potential keeps us at HOLD.

businessModel.text: Duke Energy is a regulated electric and gas utility. Key revenue streams: Regulated Electric Utilities: Generates, transmits, and distributes electricity across six states. Revenue is approved by state utility commissions; recovery of costs plus an allowed return on regulated assets (rate base). Regulated Natural Gas: Pipeline and distribution operations in Ohio, Kentucky, and the Carolinas. Similar regulatory model with approved rates. Commercial Renewables: Growing but still modest portfolio of utility-scale solar and wind assets; unregulated but contracted under long-term PPAs. The regulated utility model is highly predictable: a state commission sets rates to allow recovery of prudent costs plus ~10% return on equity. Growth comes from rate base expansion — building new substations, smart meters, grid hardening, and new generation — approved through capital expenditure programmes.

thesisEvaluation.text: Bull Case (30% weight) Aggressive rate case wins support above-average rate base growth; IRA tax credits accelerate renewable build; clean energy demand from data centres drives load growth; EPS grows to $7.50+. Target: $155–170 Base Case (50% weight) Steady rate base growth of 5–6% from grid investment; moderate rate case outcomes; regulated EPS grows in line with historical 4–5% annual guidance; dividend sustained. Target: $130–142 Bear Case (20% weight) Regulatory headwinds delay rate case approvals; interest rate sensitivity raises cost of capital for regulated utilities; load growth disappoints; EPS falls to $5.50; dividend at risk. Target: $95 Weighted Conviction Score: 70 / 100 — HOLD

keyRisks.text: Regulatory risk: Rate cases are the central earnings driver; adverse regulatory outcomes — denied investments, reduced rate base, or lower allowed returns — directly compress earnings growth. Interest rate sensitivity: Utilities are bond proxies; a rising rate environment causes re-rating to lower P/E multiples. Also increases the cost of capital for regulated operations. Load growth uncertainty: Decades of flat electricity demand are being disrupted by AI data centre power demand and industrial reshoring; the pace of change creates planning uncertainty. Carbon policy risk: State-level clean energy mandates require significant capital investment; the pace of coal retirement creates stranded asset risk in older generation. Hurricane and storm risk: Duke's service territory includes hurricane-prone areas (Carolinas, Florida); major storm events create restoration costs and regulatory recovery complexity.

risks.risk: Regulatory risk: Rate cases are the central earnings driver; adverse regulatory outcomes — denied investments, reduced rate base, or lower allowed returns — directly compress earnings growth.
Interest rate sensitivity: Utilities are bond proxies; a rising rate environment causes re-rating to lower P/E multiples. Also increases the cost of capital for regulated operations.

whoShouldOwn.text:
Own It:
• Income investors who need a 3.5% yield with high conviction of sustainability; utility dividends are among the most reliable in the S&P 500.
• Defensive portfolio positioning — utilities typically outperform in late-cycle and recessionary environments when growth slows.
• Long-term investors who want exposure to the grid modernisation and energy transition theme within a regulated, predictable structure.

Avoid It:
• Growth investors — limited capital appreciation; a utility is a yield vehicle, not a growth story.
• Those expecting rate cuts — lower rates make utilities less competitive versus bonds and typically cause multiple compression.
• Tax-inefficient accounts — utility dividends are taxed as ordinary income; tax-efficient accounts benefit from different structures.

recommendation.text: HOLD Defensive yield and regulated earnings visibility are appropriate for income-focused investors, but 20.4x P/E and 1.6x price/book near full valuation; wait for better entry. Entry / Exit Framework Conviction Score 70 / 100 — HOLD Entry Below $110 — better value on a utility at 52-week highs Add Below $95 — high conviction defensive entry Reduce / Exit Above $138 — full valuation; yield no longer compensates Time Horizon 36–60 months
